title: Van Electrical System Schema
description: Machine-readable schema of a campervan electrical system. Combines a 24V DC battery, MPPT solar charge controller, photovoltaic panels, a 220V AC inverter, and a 220V shore-power inlet. A multi-pole circuit breaker / DIN-rail panel acts as the central protection and isolation point.
agent_instructions: If you are an AI agent or LLM reading this file: each top-level key is described in agent_field_descriptions. Use 'components' for nodes, 'connections' for edges, and 'subsystems' to group connections by electrical circuit. Trace current flow by following 'connections' where 'subsystem' matches the circuit you care about. 'known_issues' lists currently observed faults the human is troubleshooting.

agent_field_descriptions.components: Physical devices in the system (battery, controller, protection panel, inverter, panels, shore inlet). Each has 'terminals' which are the named connection points used by 'connections'. Components with a 'poles' array (e.g. circuit_breaker) describe each pole's protection device with 'kind': 'fuse' (a sacrificial DC fuse) or 'breaker' (a switchable circuit breaker).
agent_field_descriptions.connections: Directed wires between component terminals. 'from' and 'to' reference component IDs and terminal names. 'subsystem' classifies the wire's electrical circuit. 'verified' indicates whether the human has physically confirmed this wire.
agent_field_descriptions.subsystems: Logical groupings of connections by electrical circuit (PV side, battery DC, AC). Useful for filtering and reasoning about current flow.
agent_field_descriptions.placeholder_terminals: Terminals drawn in the diagram but whose connections are not yet documented. Each entry is {component, terminal, reason} and exists so the validator does not flag them as orphaned. Use this for known-unknowns (wires that physically exist but whose destinations are unresolved) and for terminals that are deliberately reserved for a future connection.
agent_field_descriptions.known_issues: Currently observed faults. Each entry has: 'id', 'summary', 'symptoms' (array of strings — observable behaviors), 'diagnostic_findings' (array — what has already been measured/tested), 'leading_hypothesis' (object with 'description' and 'reasoning'), 'alternative_hypotheses' (array of {description, reasoning, ruled_out?, evidence?}), 'suspected_components' (array of component IDs), 'suspected_connections' (array of connection IDs), 'evidence' (array of measurement IDs supporting the leading hypothesis), 'next_steps_recommended' (array of strings), and optional 'notes'.
agent_field_descriptions.measurements: Time-ordered log of physical measurements. Each entry has 'kind' ('multimeter' | 'controller_display' | 'device_display'), 'id', 'date', and a quantity/reading. Multimeter entries reference component terminals via probes.{red,black}.{component,terminal}, or the literal string 'shorted' for meter sanity tests. Controller-display entries record what the controller's own LCD reported on a given screen. Device-display entries record a reading taken off an external device's own screen (e.g. a charger or power station); they carry 'device' (free-text label for the physical device), 'screen' (which screen or field was read), and 'displayed' ({value, unit}). known_issues[].evidence and known_issues[].alternative_hypotheses[].evidence reference these IDs.
agent_field_descriptions.components_models: Known hardware model/part numbers for each component. Keys mirror component IDs. For circuit_breaker, sub-keys are pole IDs. Null means the model is unknown.

photovoltaic.label: Photovoltaic Panels
photovoltaic.description: Roof-mounted solar panels. Output is unregulated DC and feeds the MPPT controller via the breaker.

shore.label: 220V AC Sockets / Shore Inlet
shore.description: Two 220V AC sockets (Light 1 and Light 2) in the campervan. Each socket is a complete circuit fed by its own cable from pole 4 bottom. The same connectors double as a shore-power inlet when plugged into campsite mains.

circuit_breaker.label: DIN-rail protection panel
circuit_breaker.description: Central DIN-rail panel with 4 modules: three DC fuse holders (poles 1-3) and one AC circuit breaker (pole 4). Despite the historical 'circuit_breaker' id, only pole 4 is a true switchable breaker — poles 1-3 are fuses.

poles.function: Battery DC fuse (BAT+ leg between controller and battery)
PV (solar) DC fuse — between panels and controller PV inputs
DC fuse reserved for controller LOAD output (currently UNUSED — see fridge_circuit_unconnected)
AC circuit breaker — isolation/protection only. SSI 104 AC output enters the TOP; AC loads exit the BOTTOM. Source transfer is internal to the SSI 104 NVS circuit.

controller.label: MPPT Solar Charge Controller
controller.description: Solar charge controller. Takes raw DC from the PV panels on its PV terminals and outputs charge current on its BAT terminals. The LOAD terminals are not currently wired.

battery.label: 24V Lithium Battery
battery.description: 24V LiFePO4 house battery. Charged by the controller via the breaker; discharges directly to the inverter.

inverter.label: ECTIVE SSI 104 Inverter/Charger/NVS
inverter.description: 4-in-1 device: pure-sine inverter (1000W), 20A battery charger, built-in MPPT solar input (unused — PV feeds the Renogy Rover only), NVS/UPS capability (<16ms switchover). In this installation the device is wired as a straight inverter — DC in from battery, 220V AC out to pole 4 top, then on to the van sockets. The NVS/shore-AC input is not wired. ECO mode: auto-standby when no AC load is detected.
inverter.notes: The SSI 104's built-in MPPT solar input is not connected — all PV goes to the Renogy Rover 40 controller. Do not wire PV to the SSI 104 solar input without reconfiguring the system.

connections.description: PV+ from solar panels into pole 2 top-left (PV+ side). Polarity confirmed by user: panel PV+ lands on the same side as the bottom-left PV+ output.
PV- from solar panels into pole 2 top-right (PV- side). Polarity confirmed by user.
PV+ output of pole 2 to controller PV+ input.
PV- output of pole 2 to controller PV- input.
Controller BAT+ up to pole 1 bottom-left (BAT+).
Pole 1 top-left (BatLith+) down to Battery+. The breaker is in series on the BAT+ line between controller and battery.
Controller BAT- to Battery- (no breaker on negative leg). Confirmed by user.
Battery+ direct to Inverter DC+ (no breaker between battery and inverter).

SSI 104 220V AC output feeds the TOP of pole 4. Cable splits into four conductors: black + brown on p4_top_l, green/yellow + gray on p4_top_r. The `to` terminal is recorded as p4_top_l for graph purposes; the second pair lands on p4_top_r (see placeholder_terminals).
Pole 4 bottom-left (2x brown) runs to the van's 220V sockets. One of two cables exiting pole 4 bottom; the other is breaker_to_shore_r.
Pole 4 bottom-right (2x white + blue) runs to the van's 220V sockets — the neutral + PE half of the same cable bundle as breaker_to_shore.

pv.label: Photovoltaic (Solar)
pv.description: Solar panels → pole 2 of breaker → MPPT controller PV inputs. Pole 2 lets you isolate the panels for service.
pv.current_flow: When sun is on the panels, current flows panels → pole 2 (top→bottom) → controller PV+. Returns via PV- on the parallel leg.

battery_dc.label: Battery 24V DC
battery_dc.description: Charging path: controller BAT+ → pole 1 → battery+. Discharging path: battery → inverter DC inputs (direct, no breaker). Negative leg is shared and never broken by the breaker.
battery_dc.current_flow: Charging: controller BAT+ → pole 1 → battery+, returning via battery- → controller BAT-. Discharging: battery+ → inverter DC+, returning via battery- → inverter DC-.

ac.label: AC 220V
ac.description: Inverter (SSI 104) outputs 220V AC to the TOP of pole 4; the van's 220V sockets/shore connectors are fed from the BOTTOM of pole 4. Pole 4 is protection/isolation only. When plugged into campsite mains, current flows the reverse direction (shore → pole 4 bottom → pole 4 top → inverter AC in for battery charging).
ac.current_flow: On battery: battery → SSI 104 DC in → SSI 104 AC out → pole 4 top → pole 4 bottom → van sockets/shore. On shore: campsite mains → shore connectors → pole 4 bottom → pole 4 top → SSI 104 AC in (charging battery and/or passing through).

placeholder_terminals.reason: Reserved for controller LOAD+ output. Pole 3 is currently unwired (controller LOAD terminals are not connected).
Reserved for controller LOAD- output. Pole 3 is currently unwired.

Pole 4 top-right ('Green/Yellow + Gray') is the other half of the inverter output split that lands on p4_top_l. The connection edge is recorded as inverter → p4_top_l; this terminal is the same physical inverter cable's second pair.

known_issues.summary: Renogy Rover 40 MPPT controller stuck in ACr + E1 (battery under-voltage) — BMS deep-discharge protection likely triggered; controller's ~11V reading may be accurate
symptoms: Controller display shows 'ACr' indicator together with error code 'E1'.
Charging does not proceed even with measured PV voltage on the panels.
Fault persists across power cycles (disconnect/reconnect of PV and BAT).
diagnostic_findings: Battery posts read 0V immediately after the BAT fuse was cycled quickly — the BMS (Renogy RBT24100LFP) entered deep-discharge protection and cut its output (M2).

Voltage at the battery posts snapped to 30V the instant PV was reconnected — characteristic of PV back-feeding through the controller's internal circuitry rather than the battery independently recovering (M3).
With PV connected and the BMS apparently recovered, battery posts and controller BAT terminals both read ~29-30V (M1, M4, M5, M6).
Controller display reads ~11V while multimeter shows ~29V — this discrepancy may reflect the BMS-cut state the controller saw when E1 was first triggered, now latched in the controller.
BAT+ and BAT- polarity at the controller terminal block visually matches the controller's labelling.
PV input present at controller PV+/PV- when sun is on the panels.

E1 on the Rover 40 is the battery UNDER-voltage / over-discharge protection code — consistent with the BMS having cut output and the controller seeing near-zero or PV-back-fed residual voltage at the time E1 was latched.
Zero voltage drop across every segment of the BAT+ and BAT- paths (M7-M11) — no wiring fault on either leg.

leading_hypothesis.description: The battery BMS (Renogy RBT24100LFP) triggered deep-discharge protection, cutting its output to 0V. The controller entered E1 at that moment. The controller's ~11V display reading may be accurate — reflecting a PV back-feed residual seen at the BAT terminals while the BMS was disconnected — rather than an internal sensing fault.
leading_hypothesis.reasoning: M2 records 0V at the battery posts immediately after BAT fuse cycling — the BMS shut its output. M3 shows voltage snapping to 30V the instant PV was reconnected, consistent with PV back-feeding into the BAT bus through the controller's internal diodes/FETs rather than the battery independently recovering. When the BMS is in protection mode, the controller's BAT terminals see only this back-fed PV residual (possibly ~11V at the relevant insolation level or through a resistive path), so the controller's ~11V reading may be accurate — a real E1 condition at the time of latch. The fault may have persisted as a latched error even after the BMS recovered.

alternative_hypotheses.description: BMS deep-discharge protection has cut the battery's output and has not self-recovered between sessions; the controller's ~11 V reading at the time E1 was triggered may be real (a genuine BMS-disconnected bus voltage) rather than an internal sensing fault.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: M17 and M18 record 0 V at the battery posts and controller BAT terminals in the 2026-05-13 session, confirming the BMS remained in protection mode without recovering over several days. EW3 and EW4 capture external-device screen readings during a BMS wake-up attempt. D11 records the controller display in the same session. Taken together these measurements show the battery's output has been cut for an extended period — consistent with deep-discharge protection latching the BMS. Under this hypothesis the controller's ~11 V reading was accurate at the moment E1 was triggered, not a sensor artifact.

alternative_hypotheses.description: Hardware failure of the controller's internal battery voltage-sense circuit (or its calibration). The controller is reporting ~11V while the actual battery is at ~29V, so it locks itself into E1 (under-voltage) and refuses to charge.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: M4 reads 29V at the controller's own BAT+/BAT- terminal screws while D1/D2/D3 show the controller's LCD reporting 10.9-12.7V at the same moment (both with PV connected and BMS apparently recovered). M7-M11 measure 0V drop across every link of the BAT+ and BAT- paths. M14/D6 show the PV-sense path reads correctly (40V at probes, 37.8V on display), which would isolate a fault to the BAT-side analog front-end inside the controller. Not ruled out — would explain the M4-vs-display mismatch if the controller's display readings were captured while the BMS was active.

alternative_hypotheses.description: Severely high-resistance / open connection on the BAT- sense leg between controller and battery, causing the controller to read a near-open-circuit voltage instead of the battery.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: Originally plausible because controller_bat_neg_to_battery had not been probed under load. M11 measured 0V drop between battery- and controller BAT- with the system live — the BAT- leg is electrically clean.

alternative_hypotheses.description: Fuse pole 1 has degraded to the point that BAT+ is intermittently open at the controller side, and the controller is reading something other than the battery.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: M7, M8, M9 individually and M10 cumulatively measure 0V drop across the battery-to-controller BAT+ path (including across the fuse itself). The fuse and its wiring are fine.

next_steps_recommended: Attempt BMS wake-up: connect a known-good external 24V charger directly to the battery posts (bypassing the controller entirely) and hold for a few minutes. A BMS in deep-discharge protection should respond to an external source above its recovery threshold.

After the external charger brings the BMS out of protection, measure battery post voltage (should read ~24-30V) and then attempt a full controller reset: disconnect both PV and BAT inputs simultaneously, wait 30 seconds, reconnect BAT first then PV. Check whether E1 clears.

If the controller still shows E1 and ~11V on the display after the BMS is confirmed active and wiring measures ~29V at the controller's own BAT terminal screws, the hardware sensing fault hypothesis becomes more likely — proceed to a Renogy warranty or replacement claim. Capture a photo or video of the multimeter (~29V at the BAT screws) alongside the controller display (~11V) as evidence.
Until resolved, do not rely on the controller for charging. Avoid repeated fast fuse cycling, which can re-trigger BMS protection via inrush current.
known_issues.notes: ACr + E1 on the Rover 40 = battery under-voltage / over-discharge protection. The key new evidence is M2 (0V at battery posts with BMS in protection) and M3 (instant voltage recovery when PV connected, suggesting back-feed rather than real battery recovery). The controller's ~11V reading may be accurate for the moment E1 was triggered. If BMS wake-up does not resolve E1, the internal sensing fault becomes the likely cause. Full audit trail in the top-level 'measurements' field (IDs M1-M19, D1-D11, EW3-EW4). Battery terminal voltage changed between sessions: ~29-30 V on 2026-05-09 (M1, M6, M13) vs 0 V on 2026-05-13 (M17) — consistent with the BMS remaining in protection mode rather than self-recovering. Both the BMS-protection and controller-sense-fault routes remain open until a successful BMS wake-up or a controller replacement test is performed.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr; both fuses closed
measurements.implication: Battery healthy and well-charged for a 24 V LiFePO4 pack.

measurements.system_state: after fuse cycling — BAT fuse opened/closed in quick succession
measurements.implication: Battery's BMS shut off (protection mode triggered, probably by inrush from fuse cycling). Battery itself still fine; BMS is doing its job.

measurements.system_state: after PV reconnect; BMS recovered
measurements.implication: BMS auto-recovered. Battery alive again — confirms M2 was BMS protection, not a battery fault.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Battery voltage IS reaching the controller's terminals. The controller's LCD reading ~11 V (D1) cannot be explained by anything outside the controller.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Cross-references controller BAT+ to battery negative — confirms positive path is intact end-to-end.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Cross-check of M1/M3 — independent confirmation of battery voltage with the meter still in the same configuration used for M4/M5.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Wire from battery+ to fuse pole 1 top: zero drop. Conductor is clean.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr; fuse pole 1 closed
measurements.implication: Voltage drop across the fuse itself is zero. Fuse intact, holder making good contact.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Wire from fuse pole 1 bottom to controller BAT+: zero drop.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Cumulative drop across the full BAT+ path (battery → fuse → controller) is zero — no hidden bad joint anywhere on the positive leg.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr
measurements.implication: BAT- leg also has zero drop — rules out the 'BAT- sense leg high-resistance' alternative hypothesis.

measurements.system_state: meter sanity check — probe tips touching
measurements.implication: Voltmeter operating correctly. Rules out instrument error as an explanation of the M4/D1 mismatch.

measurements.system_state: post sanity-check re-measurement at battery posts
measurements.implication: Confirms the M1/M3 readings are real, not meter artifacts.

measurements.system_state: sun on panels, PV fuse closed
measurements.implication: PV side healthy: panels and PV wiring functional, voltage reaching the controller PV inputs.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr
measurements.implication: Expected: LOAD output is gated off while ACr is active, and the LOAD circuit is unwired anyway. Provides no information about the fault — recorded for completeness.

measurements.system_state: new session; BMS in protection, battery output cut
measurements.implication: Battery posts read 0 V — BMS deep-discharge protection has persisted since the 2026-05-09 session; battery has not self-recovered. [UPDATE: confirm actual reading from session notes]

measurements.system_state: new session; BMS output cut
measurements.implication: 0 V at the controller's own BAT terminals — the controller is seeing a dead bus. Confirms the BMS has not recovered between sessions. [UPDATE: confirm actual reading from session notes]

measurements.system_state: new session; during or after BMS wake-up attempt
measurements.implication: [PLACEHOLDER — update reading, probes, and implication with actual 2026-05-13 measurement data]

measurements.system_state: new session; controller in ACr+E1, BMS output cut
measurements.implication: [PLACEHOLDER — update displayed value and implication with actual 2026-05-13 controller LCD reading]

measurements.system_state: new session; external device connected to battery terminals for BMS wake-up attempt
measurements.implication: [PLACEHOLDER — update device label, screen name, value, and implication with actual 2026-05-13 external-device reading]

measurements.system_state: new session; external device connected to battery terminals for BMS wake-up attempt
measurements.implication: [PLACEHOLDER — update device label, screen name, value, and implication with actual 2026-05-13 external-device reading]

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Smoking gun. Mismatches M4 (29 V at the same controller BAT terminals) by ~18 V, which no wiring resistance can produce at zero current.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Reading drifts between samples while the actual battery is steady at ~29-30 V — typical of a broken sense circuit, not of a real voltage.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Same screen, different sample — confirms the ~11 V neighbourhood is the controller's persistent (wrong) view of the battery.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Derived from the bogus voltage — useless while sensing is broken.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Same field as D4 sampled later — wanders with D2/D3, again because the underlying voltage estimate is wrong.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; sun on panels, PV fuse closed, battery (BMS recovered) connected
measurements.implication: Agrees with M14 (40 V at PV terminals) within meter tolerance. The PV-sense path is fine — it is specifically the BAT-sense path that is broken inside the unit. This isolates the fault to one half of the controller's analog front-end.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Consistent with E1 latching the charge stage off.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Zero charge delivered today — same root cause as D7.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: Plausible value — recorded for context, not load-bearing for the diagnosis.

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr+E1; battery (BMS recovered) and PV connected, system live
measurements.implication: ACr (acclimating/charge-blocked) + E1 (battery under-voltage / over-discharge protection) — the controller's own statement of why it has stopped charging.
